data.title: Eversource started at Sell equivalent by Jefferies on regulatory, credit risks
data.description:
Eversource Energy (NYSE:ES) -2.9% in Tuesday's trading as Jefferies initiates coverage with an Underperform rating and $52 price target, citing rate case risk, credit rating risk with a 4%-5% annual dilution base case, and lingering offshore wind construction risk - all without a critical data center thesis - which could culminate in dividend questions.

"With earnings, credit, dividend, regulatory, and construction risks, we see too many challenges to overcome at the current valuation," according to Jefferies analysts led by Paul Zimbardo.

The regulatory environment in Connecticut is one of the most challenging in the U.S. as evidenced by recent rate cuts for peers, Zimbardo says, noting that Eversource (ES) is extending filings and has made public statements about reducing its investment in the state; the company was required to file its Yankee Gas case, which the analyst says will be an overhang for all of 2025.

Zimbardo forecasts Eversource's (ES) payout ratio rising to 73% from 63% currently, assuming 5% growth, and with a ~$1B FY 2024 dividend payout and $1B equity issuance, "we believe investors should be discussing a dividend cut scenario."

data.title: South Korea's President lifts martial law, hours after his declaration (Update)
data.description:
South Korea's President, Yoon Suk Yeol said on Wednesday that he would lift his martial law declaration, hours after imposing it in a sudden late night address to the country, which was soon met with opposition from lawmakers who voted to block the move.

South Korea's parliament, with 190 of its 300 members present, passed a motion early on Wednesday, after the late night address, requiring the martial law declared to be lifted. The speaker of parliament declared the martial law announcement invalid.

South Korea’s won slowly started recovering after falling dramatically against the U.S. dollar on Tuesday, after the president's surprising declaration.

The U.S. Dollar (USD:KRW) was last seen trading up 0.81% against the South Korean currency, which touched a two-year low on the martial law declaration.

In an unannounced address broadcast live on the country’s YTN television, Yoon had attacked his domestic political opponents, and claimed that we would eradicate "shameless pro-North Korean anti-state forces".

Yoon did not mention any specific danger from the neighboring North Korea, which has lately been a nuclear threat to the country.

Other South Korean assets also tumbled on the news, as investors grew concerned about political turmoil and unrest in the country. The iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (NYSEARCA:EWY) was last down 1.80% after falling more than 6% in US trading, its worst intraday fall since August.

Several other ETFs also slumped on the announcement, such as the Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF (NYSEARCA:FLKR) which was down 0.79%, after dropping more than 5.6%, while Direxion Daily South Korea Bull 3X Shares ETF (KORU) was down 5.63%, after falling more than 20% after the declaration.

U.S. listed shares of Korea-based companies also reacted sharply. Shares of e-commerce company Coupang (CPNG) was down more than 3%, steel processor Posco Holdings (PKX) fell as much as 6.5%, while shares of KB Financial Group (KB) were down 1.83%.

"I declare martial law to protect the free Republic of Korea from the threat of North Korean communist forces, to eradicate the despicable pro-North Korean anti-state forces that are plundering the freedom and happiness of our people, and to protect the free constitutional order," Yoon had said.

Yoon’s surprise address shocked the country. He said he had no choice but to resort to such a measure to safeguard constitutional order, claiming that the opposition parties have taken hostage of the parliamentary process.

This story has been updated to add that the South Korea's President has lifted his martial law declaration.

data.title: Moderna down 3% as Leerink expresses concern over CMV vaccine candidate
data.description:
• Moderna's (NASDAQ:MRNA) mRNA-1647, its vaccine asset for cytomegalovirus (CMV), may not yield positive interim phase 3 data when it could be released by the end of the month, according to Leerink.
  • "MRNA expects to accrue the 81 cases necessary to trigger
    the interim analysis by YE24, but may not provide an investor update this year if the DSMB recommends not unblinding the data (due to lack of statistically significant benefit at the interim)," analyst Mani Foroohar wrote.
  • He added that given the immunological benefits of mRNA vaccinations last from four to six months and the last patient was dosed more than nine months ago, this is "diluting the impact of any benefit in the early months of the study when antibody levels in active arm patients were presumably more robust."
  • Foroohar noted that a negative readout could lead the share price to drop below $30 as it "would remove a major mid/long-term revenue driver."
  • Leerink rates Moderna at underperform. Shares are down ~3% in afternoon trading.

data.title: TPG evaluates $1.5B sale of Crunch Fitness - Reuters
data.description:
Private equity firm TPG Inc. (NASDAQ:TPG) is examining a possible sale of Crunch Fitness that may value the gym chain at $1.5 billion, including debt.

TPG is working with Jefferies on a sale process that may start during the first half of next year, according a Reuters report on Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Other buyout shops could be interested in the gym chain.

TPG and Jefferies declined to comment to Reuters. Crunch Fitness didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

TPG purchased Crunch Fitness in 2019 for an undisclosed amount.

TPG is set to present at the Goldman Sachs 2024 U.S. Financial Services Conference next Tuesday.

data.title: Procter & Gamble Q2 results could see an impact from FX volatility, Blue Yonder hack
data.description:
As a result of “significant volatility” in foreign exchange rates this year, specifically in the Brazilian real, Procter & Gamble (NYSE:PG) results for the second quarter could be adversely impacted, CFO Andre Schulten warned.

Speaking at the Morgan Stanley Global Consumer Conference, Schulten acknowledged that while Q2 has been “mostly in line with what we have expected,” the volatility recently with Brazil, Mexico and Russia are “all moving against us.”

The impact could be compounded by an incident involving Blue Yonder, the company’s transportation management provider.

Last week, Blue Yonder suffered a ransomware attack that crippled the logistics and payroll operations for P&G and other major retailers, including Starbucks (SBUX). The Scottsdale, Arizona-based Blue Yonder provides warehousing management, transportation management systems, and order management systems, among other critical functions to a wide range of industries.

The shortage of trucks might be “relatively benign,” Schulten said, but there could be some short-term shortages with a maximum impact of 2 to 3 cents to its quarterly earnings. The incident should not impact full year results, however, as Schulten gave assurances the company has “time to catch up on the shipments.”

Schulten’s remarks weighed on Procter & Gamble (PG) shares, down as much as 2%. Colgate-Palmolive (CL), Kimberly-Clark (KMB), Unilever (UL), and Kenvue (KVUE) were all lower in sympathy.

data.title: DTE Energy raised to Buy at UBS, anticipating improved Michigan outcomes
data.description:
DTE Energy (NYSE:DTE) +0.4% in Tuesday's trading as UBS upgrades to Buy from Neutral with a $143 price target, raised from $129, saying shares do not reflect the company's top quartile Michigan regulatory environment, 6.9% utility EPS growth, exposure to Section 45z tax credits, and limited need for equity.

UBS analyst William Appicelli says DTE (DTE) trades at a 7% P/E discount to the Utility group vs. the five-year 3% average premium, which implies 5.9% EPS growth implied in the stock vs. the bank's 6.4% and the company's 6%-8% EPS growth rate guidance range.

Potential catalysts include legislation that supports data center demand through exclusion of sales and use tax and a year-end call that includes increased utility capital spending and above-trend 2025 EPS guidance, while a repeal of the Inflation Reduction Act is a risk that could impact DTE Vantage section 45z credits.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P waver after hitting record highs; traders bump up odds of December rate cut
data.description:
Wall Street's major equity averages were mixed Tuesday, with investors sorting through the first in a batch of labor-market data due this week after a record-setting session a day earlier.

The S&P 500 (SP500) -0.1%, and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) +0.2%, after each finished at record highs in Monday's trade. The Dow (DJI), meanwhile, shed -0.2%.

Investors early Tuesday received the October Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey, or JOLTS, report, in what will be a busy week for jobs data that should further shape rate-cut expectations. Job openings rose more than expected in October, up 7.744M versus 7.490M consensus and the downwardly revised 7.372M print for September.

"In the context of a gradually cooling labor market on the cusp of a soft landing, today’s JOLTS report is about as good as can reasonably be expected. Layoffs remained low and openings ticked up alongside quits — signs that employer and job seeker confidence, respectively, are improving," Cory Stahle, economist at Indeed Hiring Lab, said.

Traders on Tuesday pushed up the probability of the Federal Reserve cutting its benchmark interest rate by 25 basis points at its upcoming meeting. Odds rose to ~74% from ~62% on Monday, according to the CME FedWatch tool.

﻿"The Fed’s December 18th decision will be a close one, but if the majority of voting members prioritize the employment mandate, markets should expect a cut in policy rates, supporting risk appetite," Jeffrey Roach, chief economist at LPL Financial, said.

A look a private hiring in November will arrive on Wednesday from ADP, the same day that Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is scheduled to speak in New York. The U.S. non-farm payrolls report for November is due Friday.

Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said Tuesday he expects interest rates to continue falling in the next year. Also speaking Tuesday, Federal Reserve Governor Adriana Kugler said inflation appears on a sustainable path toward the 2% target and that she will take a "meeting by meeting" approach in making policy decisions. On Monday, Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller said he was leaning toward supporting a cut in December.

In the fixed-income market Tuesday, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) reversed course and rose 1 basis point to 4.22%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 3 basis points to 4.16%.

Back in equities, eight of the 11 sectors in the S&P 500 (SP500) fell, with Industrials losing the most. Advancers were paced by Communication Services. On the Dow (DJI), Procter & Gamble (PG) was the biggest decliner, -1.8%, while Merck (MRK) topped gainers, +1.4%. Salesforce (CRM) -0.5% before the software maker released its Q3 report late Tuesday.

Elsewhere, ZJK Industrial (ZJK) +128% after the company announced a significant advancement in its collaboration with Nvidia (NVDA) focused on advanced liquid cooling systems.

In currencies, South Korea's won (KRW:USD) pared sharper losses against the U.S. dollar (USD:KRW) after South Korean lawmakers voted to block South Korean President Yoon Suk Yeol's declaration of martial law.
